title: Accounting: 3 Statements
description: Income statement, cash flow statement, and balance sheet — how they work individually and how they link together. Core to every IB interview.

sections.title: Why accounting matters in IB
sections.body: Investment bankers use financial statements every day — to value companies, build models, and advise clients on deals. If you can't read and explain the three statements fluently, you can't do the job. In interviews, nearly every question about a company starts here.
keyPoints: The three statements are the foundation of every financial model
Interviewers test them at every bank, every year
You don't need to be an accountant — you need to understand what each number means and how they connect

sections.title: The Income Statement
sections.body:
The income statement shows a company's financial performance over a period of time (a quarter or a year). It answers: how much did the company earn, and what did it cost?

Structure (top to bottom):
• Revenue (also called 'sales' or 'top line') — what customers paid
• COGS (Cost of Goods Sold) — direct costs of making the product/service
• Gross Profit = Revenue − COGS
• Operating Expenses (OpEx) — salaries, rent, marketing, R&D
• EBITDA = Gross Profit − OpEx (often shown separately as a metric)
• D&A (Depreciation & Amortization) — non-cash expense
• EBIT (Operating Income) = EBITDA − D&A
• Interest Expense — cost of debt
• EBT (Earnings Before Tax) = EBIT − Interest
• Tax — typically 20–25% of EBT
• Net Income ('bottom line') = EBT − Tax

Key insight: Net income is an accounting number, not the same as cash. Depreciation reduces net income but doesn't use cash. That's why the cash flow statement exists.
keyPoints: Revenue − COGS = Gross Profit
Gross Profit − OpEx = EBITDA
EBITDA − D&A = EBIT (Operating Income)
EBIT − Interest − Tax = Net Income
Net income ≠ cash — non-cash items and timing differences mean you need the cash flow statement

sections.title: The Cash Flow Statement
sections.body:
The cash flow statement shows the actual cash moving in and out of a business over a period. It has three sections:

1. Cash from Operations (CFO)
Starts with net income, then adjusts for things that affected profit but not cash:
• Add back D&A (non-cash expense that reduced income)
• Subtract increases in working capital assets (e.g. accounts receivable up = cash not yet collected)
• Add increases in working capital liabilities (e.g. accounts payable up = cash not yet paid)

2. Cash from Investing (CFI)
• CapEx (buying equipment) — cash outflow
• Acquisitions — cash outflow
• Asset sales — cash inflow

3. Cash from Financing (CFF)
• Debt issued — inflow
• Debt repaid — outflow
• Stock issued — inflow
• Dividends paid — outflow
• Share buybacks — outflow

Ending Cash = Beginning Cash + CFO + CFI + CFF

This ending cash balance ties directly to the cash line on the balance sheet — a key linkage point.
keyPoints: CFO: net income adjusted for non-cash items and working capital changes
Depreciation is added back in CFO because it's non-cash
Increase in AR = use of cash (earned but not collected); decrease = source
Increase in AP = source of cash (owed but not paid); decrease = use
Ending cash on the CFS = cash on the balance sheet

sections.title: The Balance Sheet
sections.body:
The balance sheet is a snapshot of what a company owns and owes at a single point in time. It must always balance:

Assets = Liabilities + Shareholders' Equity

Assets (left side):
• Current Assets: cash, accounts receivable, inventory, prepaid expenses (expected to convert to cash within a year)
• Non-current Assets: PP&E (property, plant & equipment), goodwill, intangibles, long-term investments

Liabilities (right side, top):
• Current Liabilities: accounts payable, accrued expenses, deferred revenue, short-term debt (due within a year)
• Non-current Liabilities: long-term debt, deferred tax liabilities

Shareholders' Equity (right side, bottom):
• Common stock + APIC (additional paid-in capital)
• Retained Earnings — accumulated net income minus dividends paid
• Treasury Stock — shares bought back (reduces equity)

Key insight: Equity is the residual — what's left for shareholders after all obligations. When net income is earned, it flows to retained earnings, increasing equity. When dividends are paid, retained earnings fall.
keyPoints: Assets = Liabilities + Equity — must always balance
Current assets/liabilities expected to convert to cash within 1 year
Retained earnings = prior retained earnings + net income − dividends
PP&E decreases over time due to depreciation
Goodwill appears after an acquisition and stays until impaired

sections.title: How the Three Statements Link
sections.body:
This is the most tested concept in IB accounting. Interviewers will give you a change and ask what happens to all three statements.

The flow:
1. Net income from the Income Statement → flows to the Cash Flow Statement as the starting point for CFO
2. Net income → also flows to the Balance Sheet as an increase in Retained Earnings (within Equity)
3. D&A from the Income Statement → added back in the CFO section of the Cash Flow Statement
4. CapEx from the Cash Flow Statement (CFI) → increases PP&E on the Balance Sheet
5. Depreciation from the Income Statement → reduces PP&E on the Balance Sheet over time
6. Changes in working capital (AR, inventory, AP) → appear in CFO on the Cash Flow Statement and change current assets/liabilities on the Balance Sheet
7. Ending Cash from the Cash Flow Statement = Cash on the Balance Sheet (the key tie-out)

The balance sheet always balances because:
Beginning Equity + Net Income − Dividends = Ending Equity
And Assets = Liabilities + Ending Equity

Practice transaction: 'Depreciation goes up by $10'
• IS: EBIT down $10, Net Income down $6 (at 40% tax)
• CFS: starts $6 lower (net income), add back $10 D&A → CFO up $4, cash up $4
• BS: PP&E down $10, retained earnings down $6, cash up $4 → Assets: net −$6; Equity: −$6 → balanced
keyPoints: Net income → retained earnings (BS) and starting point of CFS
D&A: expense on IS, added back in CFO, reduces PP&E on BS
Ending cash on CFS = cash on BS — the key tie-out
Any transaction that hits the IS changes equity through retained earnings
BS always balances: check that ΔAssets = ΔLiabilities + ΔEquity

sections.title: Key Accounting Concepts
sections.body:
A few concepts you must know cold:

Accrual Accounting: Revenue is recorded when earned (goods delivered / service rendered), not when cash is received. Expenses are recorded when incurred, not when paid. All public companies use this.

Revenue Recognition (ASC 606): Revenue is recognized when control of the good or service transfers to the customer. Key test: has the performance obligation been satisfied?

Non-cash Items: Depreciation and amortization reduce income but don't use cash. Stock-based compensation (SBC) is also non-cash. All three are added back in CFO.

Deferred Revenue: Cash received before revenue is earned. A liability (you owe the service). When delivered: liability down, revenue up.

Working Capital: Current Assets − Current Liabilities (often ex-cash and ex-debt). Higher working capital = more cash tied up in operations.

EBITDA vs Net Income: EBITDA strips out D&A, interest, and taxes — it's used to compare operating performance across companies with different capital structures. Net income includes all of these.

Free Cash Flow: CFO − CapEx. The cash a company generates after investing to maintain/grow the business. What's actually available to debt and equity holders.
keyPoints: Accrual: record revenue when earned, expenses when incurred
Revenue recognized when performance obligation satisfied (control transferred)
D&A, SBC are non-cash — reduce income, added back in CFO
Working capital increase = more cash tied up = use of cash in CFO
FCF = CFO − CapEx (simple definition; unlevered adds back interest)
